content: Stable CAD diagnosis: clinical assessment (pretest probability based on age, sex, symptom typicality), resting ECG (may show prior MI, LVH, ST-T changes), echocardiogram (wall motion abnormalities, EF). Functional testing for ischemia: exercise ECG (if able to exercise and interpretable ECG), stress echocardiography (exercise or dobutamine), nuclear perfusion imaging (SPECT or PET with exercise or vasodilator), stress cardiac MRI. Anatomic testing: coronary CT angiography (CCTA) excellent for ruling out CAD in low-intermediate risk patients (high negative predictive value), can characterize plaque; invasive coronary angiography gold standard, allows immediate PCI if needed. ISCHEMIA trial showed no difference in CV outcomes between initial invasive vs conservative strategy in stable CAD with moderate-severe ischemia, supporting medical therapy first-line. FFR (fractional flow reserve) or iFR during angiography determines physiologic significance of intermediate lesions (FFR ≤0.80 indicates ischemia-producing stenosis).
